policy_statement: The City will lead creation of a regional coalition in the five-county MSA to support EV adoption with government, business, and community stakeholders.
mechanism_description: Creating a regional EV coalition coordinates stakeholders and reduces adoption barriers, enabling higher EV uptake and associated transport emissions reductions over time.
primary_causal_pathway: Coordination/market enabling → ↑ EV adoption → ↓ ICE fuel combustion → ↓ GHG emissions → ↓ atmospheric forcing
causal_mechanism_detail:
(1) Change in human/system activity: The city convenes and leads a regional coalition to align plans and actions that enable EV adoption (e.g., coordinated charging rollout, harmonized permitting, joint outreach, aligned incentives, fleet commitments).  
(2) Mediating process: Reduced transaction costs and adoption barriers plus improved network effects (more charging availability, clearer standards, higher consumer confidence) increases EV purchases and use.  
(3) Climate-relevant outcome: Increased electrified travel displaces fossil fuel combustion in the transport sector, lowering lifecycle GHG emissions (magnitude depends on grid mix but directionally reduces emissions in typical contexts).
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is reduced transportation GHG emissions from accelerated EV adoption that displaces gasoline/diesel vehicle miles.
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Coalition-building is an enabling intervention whose main downstream effect is higher EV adoption leading to lower tailpipe (and typically lifecycle) GHG emissions—i.e., mitigation.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While EVs can improve local air quality and energy security, the climate-relevant “single biggest” effect of the mechanism is emissions reduction from replacing internal combustion driving with electrified driving.  
3) Inherent causal chain for each secondary: None—resource efficiency (less energy per mile) or adaptation co-benefits are not guaranteed across all instances and depend on vehicle type, grid carbon intensity, and complementary measures.  
4) Edge cases applied: If the grid is very carbon-intensive or EV adoption increases vehicle miles traveled, net GHG benefits can be smaller; however, the intended and generally dominant climate pathway remains mitigation via electrification.  
5) Confirm labels apply to ALL instances: Any policy using this mechanism (coalition-building to enable EV adoption) is fundamentally designed to increase EV uptake; the consistent climate-relevant intent and typical outcome is transport emissions mitigation.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: If coalition efforts primarily focus on economic development/industry growth without materially increasing EV adoption, climate impact may be indirect or negligible; also, net GHG benefits vary with grid mix and induced demand, but these do not change the mechanism’s dominant mitigation pathway.
key_indicators: "lead in creating a regional coalition"; "support EV adoption"; "within the fivecounty MSA"
co_benefits: Improved regional air quality and public health; reduced transportation fuel costs; economic development/workforce opportunities; reduced transportation noise
instance_edge_case_notes: Coalition formation could be paired with future instruments (incentives, procurement, infrastructure investment), but the text here only establishes coordination/leadership, so it is classified as a planning/strategy enabling instrument.
